Q1: Honestly, my study routine was pretty unconventional. I'm 27 and I've learned that I need to physically do things to understand them, so I'd carve out chunks of time—usually around 23 hours a week—where I could work through problems hands-on, practice applications, and move around while studying. I rarely sat at a computer for long stretches. Instead, I'd download what I needed, print things out, and work through material at my kitchen table or even while pacing my apartment.
Q2: My pattern definitely varied based on what was due and what else was happening in my life. With 11 online courses to juggle, I couldn't stick to a rigid schedule even if I wanted to. Deadlines absolutely drove when I'd intensify my focus on particular subjects. Some weeks I'd be completely absorbed in one course, other weeks I'd have to spread myself thin across several.
Q3: Usually I logged in when I absolutely had to—to check assignment requirements, submit something, or see if there were announcements I'd missed. I wasn't someone who browsed the LMS casually. My anxiety about keeping up with everything meant I'd sometimes avoid logging in because seeing all the tasks would overwhelm me, so I'd only go in with a specific purpose.
Q4: Not really, to be honest. The LMS never became a comfortable routine for me. I found it more stressful than helpful, so I'd get what I needed and get out. The gamified tasks they introduced were mildly interesting at first, but the novelty wore off quickly and didn't change my fundamental relationship with the platform.
Q5: The video lectures were genuinely useful—I watched over two hours' worth because I could download them and watch at my own pace, pausing to take notes or try things out myself. Discussion posts were also valuable when peers shared practical examples or different perspectives. Anything I could take offline and work with kinesthetically served me better than static reading materials.
Q6: I ignored a lot of the supplementary readings and some of the discussion threads that felt like busywork. If something didn't seem directly connected to assessments or practical application, I'd skip it. Time was precious with my course load, and my anxiety made me prioritize ruthlessly. Some resources just felt like they were there to pad out the course rather than genuinely help.
Q7: An activity felt worth my time if I could see a direct connection to something I'd be assessed on or if it helped me actually understand a concept I was struggling with. I also valued anything that let me engage physically—simulations, practice problems, hands-on exercises. If it was just passive consumption without clear purpose, I'd feel bored and disengaged.
Q8: Optional activities felt like luxuries I couldn't afford. With my stress levels and the sheer volume of required work across 11 courses, optional meant 'probably not happening.' Required activities at least had clear stakes, which motivated me even when I was anxious. Optional ones made me feel guilty for not doing them, which just added to my stress.
Q9: Absolutely, this was my primary mode of studying. I'd download PDFs, print lecture slides, and work through problems on paper. I used external YouTube tutorials, textbooks I'd bought separately, and even created physical flashcards. The LMS was just a portal to get materials—my real learning happened away from screens, where I could move and engage physically.
Q10: Most of my meaningful studying wouldn't show up in the LMS at all. I'd spend hours working through practice problems at my desk, teaching concepts out loud to myself, or meeting with a study group in person. My exam scores reflect learning that happened entirely offline. The LMS would show me as barely engaged when I was actually deeply immersed in the material.
Q11: All the time. I'd access a video or document once, download it immediately, and never return to it on the platform. My video watch time in the LMS is probably a fraction of how much I actually watched those lectures. I preferred having everything locally so I could study without internet distractions and work at my own pace.
Q12: There were definitely periods when my anxiety spiked and engaging felt impossible. When multiple deadlines converged or when I felt behind, I'd sometimes freeze up and avoid the course entirely for a few days. The boredom also hit hard mid-semester when the initial motivation faded and everything felt repetitive. My high stress made even small tasks feel monumental.
Q13: Constantly. My workload across 11 courses meant I was always triaging. Fatigue was real—I'm not 20 anymore, and balancing everything takes more energy than it used to. When I was exhausted or overwhelmed, the LMS became something I avoided because logging in meant confronting everything I hadn't done yet. Competing responsibilities always won over casual engagement.
Q14: Yes, sometimes I'd scroll through materials with my mind completely elsewhere, just going through the motions. My eyes would pass over text but nothing would stick. This usually happened when I was burned out but felt guilty about not studying. Those sessions were basically wasted time that probably showed up as 'engagement' in the system.
Q15: I postponed discussion posts constantly, even though I knew participation mattered. The anxiety of putting my thoughts out publicly, combined with feeling bored by topics that didn't grab me, made them easy to delay. I'd tell myself I'd do them later when I felt more inspired, but that inspiration rarely came. My assignment completion rate reflects this pattern.
Q16: Deadlines were everything. They were the only thing that could cut through my boredom and anxiety enough to force action. I'd have intense bursts of activity right before due dates, which probably looked erratic in the logs. Without deadlines, I'd drift. They created necessary pressure, even though that pressure also fed my stress.
Q17: Feedback definitely affected me, though not always positively. Good feedback motivated me to engage more with that instructor's materials. Harsh or unclear feedback made me want to avoid the course entirely. My resilience helped me bounce back eventually, but there was usually a delay where I'd need to process my emotional reaction before re-engaging.
Q18: The structure felt overwhelming with so many courses running simultaneously. I appreciated when modules were clearly organized and expectations were explicit. Vague instructions or scattered materials increased my anxiety significantly. The gamified elements were a nice attempt but didn't fundamentally change how I approached the course—they felt a bit patronizing, honestly.
Q19: Clear rubrics and well-organized modules made engagement easier. What made it harder was when important information was buried in multiple places or when the interface required too many clicks to find what I needed. The low attendance expectations actually worked against me—without that external structure, I had to generate all my own motivation, which was exhausting.
Q20: I think those students might be searching for something—maybe they're confused about requirements, anxious about what they're missing, or trying to figure out what's actually important. High activity doesn't mean productive activity. They could also be procrastinating by browsing instead of doing the actual work, which I've definitely been guilty of.
Q21: That's basically me. My LMS engagement was minimal, but I performed reasonably well because my real learning happened offline. I'm a kinesthetic learner—I need to physically work through things, not just click around a platform. The LMS captured maybe 20% of my actual study effort. My exam scores came from hours of hands-on practice that left no digital trace.
Q22: Course logs can show patterns—when someone logs in, what they click on, submission times. But they completely miss the quality of engagement, offline studying, emotional state, and whether someone actually understood anything. They can't capture me pacing my apartment reciting concepts or working through problems on paper. They show behavior, not learning.
Q23: Being engaged means I'm genuinely curious and invested in understanding the material, not just completing tasks. It means I'm thinking about course concepts even when I'm not actively studying—connecting ideas while cooking dinner or noticing real-world applications. True engagement is mental and emotional investment, which looks nothing like login frequency.
Q24: The LMS would never show how much I struggled with anxiety while still pushing through, or how I'd spend hours studying in ways that worked for my learning style. It wouldn't capture my resilience—how I'd bounce back after difficult periods. It also misses that my boredom wasn't disengagement; it was frustration with a platform that didn't match how I actually learn best.
